content.text: The memory hierarchy in Claude Code is the layered system that determines
— so that the most contextually relevant and specific instructions shape Claude's behavior without flooding the context window.

Think of the memory hierarchy as concentric rings of authority, from widest scope to narrowest. Each ring adds instructions to Claude's context window at session start, layered on top of the previous one. The outermost ring (organization-wide policy) is always present; the innermost ring (your per-project local file) is the last thing Claude reads, giving it the highest contextual weight.
all discovered files are concatenated, not overridden
. Nothing is silently ignored unless you explicitly exclude it. Instructions from broader scopes stack with instructions from narrower scopes.
Claude Code defines four distinct scopes for
files, loaded in this order at session start — from broadest to most specific:
Within each directory encountered during the walk,
, so your personal notes at a given level are the last thing Claude reads at that level.
Directory Tree Walking: How Files Actually Load
When you launch Claude Code in a directory (e.g.,
the directory tree from your working directory toward the filesystem root, collecting
at every level it encounters. For
concatenated into context in reverse order
— from the filesystem root down to your working directory. So the actual injection order into Claude's context is:

/etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md       ← Loaded first (least specific)
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
foo/CLAUDE.md
foo/bar/CLAUDE.md                ← Loaded last (most specific)
foo/bar/CLAUDE.local.md          ← Very last at this level
This means more specific instructions are read
, giving them the highest contextual prominence — exactly analogous to how CSS specificity or Python's MRO resolves conflicts.

loaded at session start. They are loaded lazily — only when Claude reads a file inside that subdirectory during the session. This is a deliberate design decision: a monorepo might have dozens of nested
files, and loading all of them upfront would flood the context window. Only the files relevant to the current task's files are ever injected.
This also has an important implication for
files survive compaction (Claude re-reads them from disk after compacting), but nested subdirectory
automatically re-inject — they reload only the next time Claude reads a file in that subdirectory.
For larger projects, you can further subdivide instructions beyond a monolithic
files in this directory are discovered recursively, so you can organize by team, feature area, or concern:

your-project/
├── .claude/
│   ├── CLAUDE.md
│   └── rules/
│       ├── code-style.md       # Always loaded
│       ├── testing.md          # Always loaded
│       ├── frontend/
│       │   └── react.md        # Always loaded
│       └── api-rules.md        # Path-scoped (only when Claude reads API files)
frontmatter load at session start with the same priority as
frontmatter field load conditionally — only when Claude reads a file matching the specified glob pattern:

---
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
---

# API Development Rules
- All endpoints must include input validation
- Use the standard error response format

This path-scoped conditional loading is the primary tool for keeping context lean on large codebases. TypeScript API rules only enter the context window when Claude is actually working with TypeScript API files.

All worktrees and subdirectories within the same git repository share a single auto memory directory, since the
path is derived from the git repository root — not the specific subdirectory you launched Claude from.
Putting it all together, the effective load order from first-loaded (lowest contextual weight) to last-loaded (highest contextual weight) is:
files — walking up from working directory to repo root

Patterns match against absolute file paths and merge across settings layers (user, project, local, managed). However,

The hierarchy has four scope levels: managed policy → user → project → local. All matching files are
in that order; more specific files are read last and carry higher contextual weight.

Understanding the internals of memory hierarchy helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with memory hierarchy has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying memory hierarchy in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Memory Hierarchy is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
